visits.text: Drive to Aksu-Zhabagly Nature Reserve, Central Asia's oldest—established in 1926. Aksu Canyon, 500 m deep, is one of the most impressive in the western Tian Shan. The limestone cliffs shelter colonies of griffon vultures and bearded vultures. You might also spot ibex or, with considerable luck, traces of a snow leopard.

Second day in the reserve to explore the valley's petroglyphs—Bronze Age rock carvings depicting ibex, deer, and hunting scenes. Less famous than Tamgaly, these petroglyphs sit in an intact natural setting with no tourist facilities.

activities.text: Hike in the reserve—several marked trails traverse alpine meadows covered with wild tulips in spring (Greig's tulip, ancestor of our garden tulips, originates from this region). A reserve guide is mandatory. Plan for half a day.

Hike to the high-altitude meadows where Kazakh shepherds still pitch their yurts in summer. If you visit between April and June, the meadows burst with red and yellow wild tulips—one of the few places on Earth where you can see these flowers in their original habitat. Several wild species from this region, including Greig's tulip, count among the ancestors of our garden tulips.

days_plan.itin_title: Kazakhstan Road Trip: Mangystau, Caspian Sea & the Silk Road
Kazakhstan Road Trip: Mangystau, Caspian Sea & the Silk Road

visits.text: Arrive in Aktau, Kazakhstan's only port city on the Caspian Sea. Built in the 1960s in the middle of the desert for uranium extraction, it has a unique Soviet orthogonal layout where districts are numbered rather than named. Stroll along the seafront: the Caspian stretches endlessly like an ocean, with spectacular sunsets over the water.

Visit Aktau's lighthouse and the small memorial park nearby. The city serves as the launching point for all expeditions into Mangystau—take advantage of this last brush with civilization to stock up on supplies, water, and fuel.

Return to Aktau (long day of driving). En route, stop at Karagie Depression—Kazakhstan's lowest point, 132 m below sea level. The panorama over this vast arid basin is dizzying. It's the world's fifth-deepest depression.

activities.text: Swim in the Caspian Sea—the water is warmer than you'd imagine, and the fine sandy beaches north of Aktau are rarely crowded. In the evening, sample fresh Caspian fish at a waterfront restaurant—grilled sturgeon or pike-perch.
Rest day in Aktau after 5 days of desert camping. Enjoy a hot shower, a good meal, and the Caspian beach. You've earned it.

days_plan.itin_title: Kazakhstan Road Trip: Mangystau, Caspian Sea & the Silk Road
Kazakhstan Road Trip: Mangystau, Caspian Sea & the Silk Road

places.name: Almaty

visits.text: Begin with Zenkov Cathedral, at the heart of Panfilov Park. This 56-metre-tall Orthodox church was built between 1904 and 1907 from Tian Shan spruce timber - practically without nails, using an ingenious system of interlocking beams with rolling hooks. When the devastating 1911 Kebin earthquake struck the region, it remained standing while countless buildings collapsed. Architect Zenkov, who had overseen construction and added earthquake-resistant modifications, wrote that the bell tower swayed like a treetop in wind, bent but intact. Step inside to see Khludov's gilded iconostasis - you won't find anything comparable in Central Asia.

Right next door, the Museum of Folk Musical Instruments occupies an elegant 1908 wooden building designed by that same Zenkov. You'll find over 1,000 instruments representing 60 types, including dombras and kobyzes that belonged to celebrated composers. Some date back to the 17th century. If you catch the Sazgen ensemble, stay - they perform live.

Head to the Central State Museum of Kazakhstan, the country's finest museum. In the entrance hall, a life-size replica of the Golden Man greets you - this 5th-4th century BC Saka warrior discovered near Issyk in 1969, covered in gold plaques, has become a national symbol. Don't miss the Gold Hall with its 2,500-year-old Scythian ornaments. Allow at least 90 minutes.

Head for Medeu, 15 km southeast of the centre. This open-air ice rink is the world's highest - 1,691 metres altitude. The skating venue has existed since 1951, but the current stadium dates from 1972. Over 120 world speed skating records have been set here thanks to exceptional ice quality at altitude. Even outside skating season, the site merits the trip for the panoramic view of the snow-capped Zailiysky Alatau peaks.

From Medeu, take the cable car up to Shymbulak resort at 2,200-2,500 m altitude. The gondola offers plunging views over the valley. At the summit, even in summer, you can hike the ridges and observe peaks over 4,000 m surrounding Almaty. The contrast between the modern city below and the glaciers above is striking.

Late afternoon, ascend Kok Tobe by cable car from Dostyk Avenue - six minutes above Almaty's rooftops to reach 1,100 m. At the top, the 372 m TV tower (the world's tallest tubular steel structure, designed to withstand the most powerful earthquakes) dominates the park. Stay for sunset - the view of the city lighting up with mountains as backdrop is the finest in Almaty.

Today, head for Big Almaty Lake - a glacial lake at 2,511 m altitude, just 15 km south of the centre. The lake stretches 1.6 km long and changes colour with the seasons: vivid turquoise in July-August when glacial melt peaks, deep green in autumn, pale grey in spring. The hike from the checkpoint runs about 7 km one way (300 m elevation gain) - allow 2.5 hours going up. Warning: swimming is forbidden, as this is Almaty's drinking water reservoir. Bring water and snacks, there's nothing en route.

Head for Kaindy Lake - the "sunken forest". This 400 m long lake formed in 1911 after an earthquake triggered a landslide, creating a natural dam at 2,000 m altitude. Tian Shan spruce trunks still protrude from the emerald water like ghostly masts. It's Kazakhstan's most photographed image. Warning: the access track (12 km from Saty) requires 4x4 - there's a river crossing. Otherwise, rent a vehicle with driver in Saty.

Day excursion to the Tamgaly petroglyphs - a UNESCO site since 2004, 160 km northwest of Almaty (about 2.5 hours driving). In Tamgaly Gorge, some 5,000 rock carvings cover the dark rocks - the oldest date back 3,500 years to the Bronze Age. You'll see solar deities with rays around their heads, dancing shamans, mounted warriors and animal herds. The most recent carvings date from the 20th century - Kazakh shepherds drew Soviet vehicles. The contrast between the dark Bronze Age engravings and the lighter medieval ones is striking.

activities.text: End the day at the Green Bazaar - the two-level covered market exudes authentic Central Asian atmosphere. Sample dried fruits, kurt (dried cheese balls), mountain honey and loose spices. Even if you don't buy anything, it's worth visiting for the ambience and colours alone.
Stroll along pedestrian Jibek Joly street (Almaty's Arbat) - café terraces and craft shops line the way. In the evening, locals gather here for a leisurely walk.

Treat yourself to a session at the Arasan Baths, one of the last great Soviet public baths still operating. The circular building offers Russian, Finnish and Oriental sections. Allow about 2 hours to enjoy the hammam, sauna and cold plunge - a local ritual not to be missed.

Hike round-trip to Big Almaty Lake via the pipeline trail or the winding road. Start early - before 8am - to avoid heat and enjoy the perfect reflection of peaks in the still morning water. Sovetov Peak (4,317 m), Ozyorny (4,110 m) and Tourist (3,954 m) frame the lake. Pack a warm layer even in summer - it's 10-15°C cooler than in the city.
Return to Almaty (about 4-5 hours driving via the M-39). En route, if time allows, stop at Black Canyon or Moon Canyon - two lesser-known but photogenic secondary gorges along the road.

Explore the 48 petroglyph groups spread across different side canyons. The main canyon concentrates the densest carvings and what archaeologists believe are sacrificial altars. Bronze Age and Iron Age burial mounds also dot the site. An on-site guardian can direct you - the trail marked with arrows takes about 1.5 hours to complete the circuit. Bring water and a hat, there's no shade whatsoever.

days_plan.itin_title: Kazakhstan Road Trip: Almaty, Tian Shan Canyons and Alpine Lakes
Kazakhstan Road Trip: Almaty, Tian Shan Canyons and Alpine Lakes
Kazakhstan Road Trip: Almaty, Tian Shan Canyons and Alpine Lakes
Kazakhstan Road Trip: Almaty, Tian Shan Canyons and Alpine Lakes
Kazakhstan Road Trip: Almaty, Tian Shan Canyons and Alpine Lakes

map_keywords: Museum of Folk Musical Instruments Almaty

visits.text: Drive to Altyn-Emel National Park - inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage list in 2023 as part of the "Cold Winter Deserts of Turan". At over 535,000 hectares, it's one of Kazakhstan's largest parks. Stop first at the park office in Basshi to purchase your permit and collect route keys. Head for the Besshatyr burial mounds: this Saka necropolis from the 6th-4th century BC contains 31 kurgans, the largest measuring 104 m in diameter and 17 m high. Around them, circles of standing stones recall Stonehenge - their function remains a mystery.

Continue to the Singing Dune - a barchan 150 m high and 3 km long, formed by wind carrying sand from the Ili River banks. In dry weather, when the sand slides, the dune emits a deep hum resembling an organ or distant bomber. Only about thirty "singing" dunes exist worldwide. Climb to the summit for the view over the Ili valley - but know the slope is steep and the sand burns in summer.

activities.text: Watch for Przewalski's horses on the park's plains - Altyn-Emel harbours one of the last populations living in natural habitat. You might also spot Persian gazelles (dzheiran) or onagers (kulan). The park concentrates 40% of Kazakhstan's fauna.

days_plan.itin_title: Kazakhstan Road Trip: Almaty, Tian Shan Canyons and Alpine Lakes

places.name: Astana

visits.text: Morning flight Almaty → Astana (about 1.5 hours). Begin with Bayterek Tower, the capital's symbol - 97 m tall, crowned with a 22 m diameter golden sphere representing the egg of Samruk, the sacred bird of Kazakh mythology. Take the lift to 86 m for panoramic views over the left bank. Inside, you'll find President Nazarbayev's handprint in a wooden globe.

Walk down Nurzhol Boulevard - the monumental axis of new Astana. Each building competes with futuristic forms. Visit the National Museum of Kazakhstan, the country's largest: the exhibition covers history from the Bronze Age to independence, with a poignant section on the Stalinist Great Famine. The Gold Hall presents spectacular nomadic ornaments.

Hazrat Sultan Mosque is Kazakhstan's and Central Asia's largest - it can accommodate up to 10,000 worshippers. Inaugurated in 2012, its main dome rises 51 m high and 28 m in diameter, and its four minarets reach 77 m. The classical Islamic style is enhanced with traditional Kazakh ornaments. The interior is luminous and peaceful - visit in the morning for the best light.

The Palace of Peace and Reconciliation - a glass and steel pyramid 62 m tall designed by Norman Foster, inaugurated in 2006. It houses a 1,500-seat opera house, conference rooms and a winter garden. Ascend to the summit for the view and circular stained glass windows.

Khan Shatyr is a giant translucent tent 150 m tall, also designed by Foster. Under its ETFE dome, a shopping centre houses boutiques, restaurants and - surprise - a tropical beach with pool on the top floor. The idea of an artificial beach in the midst of the Kazakh steppes rather sums up Astana's ambition.

Enjoy the morning at Burabay for a final walk - Lake Shchuchye, in the park's north, is less crowded and offers fine sandy beaches surrounded by pines. The water is fresh but clean - locals swim here all summer. Marked hiking trails crisscross the surrounding hills, with panoramas over the infinite steppe beyond the forests.

activities.text: Stroll Nurzhol Boulevard in the evening - the fountains and illuminated buildings transform the avenue into a light show. Sample beshbarmak (boiled meat over flat noodles) at a Kazakh restaurant - it's the national dish, and Astana is the best place to discover it.

Visit the EXPO 2017 complex, converted into a cultural and scientific centre. The giant Nur Alem sphere (80 m diameter) houses an interactive museum on future energies. The EXPO district gives a good sense of how rapidly Astana has transformed in just 25 years.

Return to Astana (about 3 hours by road, or 2.5 hours by modern train). End of road trip - departure flight from Astana International Airport. If you have time before your flight, visit the ALZHIR memorial 35 km west of Astana - a former camp for wives of political prisoners, now transformed into a place of remembrance.

days_plan.itin_title: Kazakhstan Road Trip: Almaty, Tian Shan Canyons and Alpine Lakes
Kazakhstan Road Trip: Almaty, Tian Shan Canyons and Alpine Lakes
Kazakhstan Road Trip: Almaty, Tian Shan Canyons and Alpine Lakes

map_keywords: Palace of Peace and Reconciliation Astana

visits.text: You arrive at Kazakhstan's most spectacular site: Boszhira. This gorge at the base of the Ustyurt Plateau was once the floor of the Tethys Ocean—the white limestone cliffs reach 250 m high. The "Fangs" of Boszhira—two isolated rock pillars—are Mangystau's most iconic image, reproduced on the 1,000 tenge banknote. The site is immense, and you can spend hours here encountering no more than a handful of people.

Climb to the viewpoint on the plateau rim—the panorama over the entire valley, with its white formations and colored streaks, looks more like another planet than Earth. The cliffs shift color with the light: beige in full daylight, pink at sunrise, golden at sunset. It's the country's best photo spot.

activities.text: Hike to the Boszhira Fangs from the plateau—the descent is steep but manageable in 1.5 hours. Watch the cliff edges—the limestone is crumbly and cracks run across the surface. Camp at the base of the Fangs for tomorrow's sunrise.

days_plan.itin_title: Kazakhstan Road Trip: Mangystau, Caspian Sea & the Silk Road

visits.text: Long drive to Burabay (about 5-6 hours via Astana, or directly from Karaganda). But arrival rewards you: after hundreds of kilometres of scorched steppe, a wall of pine trees suddenly rises. Kazakhs call Burabay "the pearl of Kazakhstan" or "Kazakh Switzerland". The national park of 129,935 hectares harbours 14 lakes, pine and birch forests, and granite rock formations that appeared 5 million years ago.

Walk around Lake Borovoye - the park's largest. In its midst, Zhumbaktas rock ("mysterious stone") rises from the water like an island. Depending on the angle, it resembles a young girl's face, a woman's, then an old lady's. Locals rent kayaks to approach it. To the south, Mount Okzhetpes ("that the arrow cannot reach") dominates the lake - its name comes from a legend of a princess and suitors.

activities.text: Climb to the Blue Bay viewpoint via the wooden staircase - 15 minutes suffices and the view embraces Lake Borovoye, Zhumbaktas rock, Mount Okzhetpes and Lakes Bolshoye and Maloye Chebache. It's the park's finest panorama.

Wander through the dancing birches forest - trunks twisted by wind that seem to sketch a choreography. According to legend, young girls dancing in a clearing were transformed into trees when the khan surprised them. Magical atmosphere late in the day.

days_plan.itin_title: Kazakhstan Road Trip: Almaty, Tian Shan Canyons and Alpine Lakes

visits.text: Before leaving Altyn-Emel, explore the Aktau and Katutau mountains. The Aktau ("White Mountains") are an open-air geological museum - layers of sedimentary rock 25 to 30 million years old, deposited on the floor of the ancient Tethys Ocean, form bands of white, pink, orange and red. Remains of giant rhinoceroses, crocodiles and turtles have been discovered here. The Katutau, right nearby, preserve eroded volcanic lava formations sculpted into natural forms.

Drive to Charyn Canyon - 150 km long, up to 300 m deep, carved by the Charyn River over 12 million years. Arrive late afternoon for sunset: the red sandstone walls take on absolutely spectacular scarlet and orange hues. Settle in at the canyon rim for the night - accommodation is basic but the silence of the steppe at dusk is worth its weight in gold.

activities.text: If time permits, descend into the Valley of Castles late afternoon - the red sandstone columns sculpted by wind and water resemble medieval towers. The descent covers 2 km, allow 1 hour one way including photo stops. The low evening light glorifies the formations.

days_plan.itin_title: Kazakhstan Road Trip: Almaty, Tian Shan Canyons and Alpine Lakes

visits.text: Drive to Karaganda (about 3 hours from Astana), then continue 36 km southwest to Dolinka village. The KarLag Museum occupies the former administrative building - a deceptively elegant Stalinist neoclassical façade. Between 1931 and 1959, up to one million prisoners passed through this network of labour camps covering 17,600 km² - equivalent to France's area, according to local guides. The two-floor exhibition shows reconstructed cells, prisoner identity photos and declassified documents. Allow at least 2 hours - it's Kazakhstan's finest gulag museum.

Back in Karaganda, visit the Ecomuseum - it traces the history of this city born from coal and camps. The blend of massive Soviet architecture and green parks gives Karaganda a strange atmosphere, between memory and renewal.

activities.text: Stroll Karaganda's centre in the evening. The city has retained an unapologetically Soviet character - broad avenues, massive buildings, communist-era statues. Kazakhs have a saying: "Have you been to Karaganda?" - a question one didn't ask, because the answer often implied the gulag.

days_plan.itin_title: Kazakhstan Road Trip: Almaty, Tian Shan Canyons and Alpine Lakes

places.name: Kolsai

visits.text: Rise early to descend into the Valley of Castles before organized tours arrive (around 10-11am in summer). The 3.5 km trail winds between 100 m tall red sandstone pillars sculpted into tower and keep shapes. At the bottom, the Charyn River flows among Sogdian ash trees - a relict species from the Tertiary era, remnant of a forest that once covered the Tian Shan foothills. If you search the rock for fossils, you might find 12-million-year-old shells.

The Kolsai Lakes are three alpine lakes stacked at 1,870 m, 2,250 m and 2,650 m altitude in the Tian Shan mountains. The first lake, the most accessible, lies 9 km from Saty on a paved road. Its turquoise waters reflect fir trees and snow-capped peaks - it's one of Kazakhstan's most beautiful images. Take time to sit by the shore, the silence is total.

activities.text: Drive to Saty village and the Kolsai Lakes (about 2-2.5 hours from Charyn). Settle into a family guesthouse - the home-cooked dinners with local produce are one of the pleasures of staying here. Saty is an authentic mountain village without a single tourist restaurant.

Hike from Kolsai Lake 1 to Kolsai Lake 2 - the trail climbs through fir forests and alpine meadows for about 6 km and 400 m elevation gain. Allow 5 to 7 hours round-trip. The second lake, wilder and less frequented, amply rewards the effort. If you're fit, continue to the third lake - but plan for a full day.

days_plan.itin_title: Kazakhstan Road Trip: Almaty, Tian Shan Canyons and Alpine Lakes
Kazakhstan Road Trip: Almaty, Tian Shan Canyons and Alpine Lakes

map_keywords: Charyn Canyon Valley of Castles Kazakhstan

places.name: Mangystau

visits.text: First day in the Mangystau desert. First stop: Shakpak-Ata underground mosque, carved into the limestone rock several centuries ago. The interior is surprisingly spacious, with sculpted columns and prayer niches. It's one of the region's oldest Sufi sites—pilgrims still come here.

Head to Torysh Valley of Balls—a field of perfect spherical concretions, some several meters in diameter, scattered across a desert plateau. The effect is surreal—you feel like you're standing on a gigantic stone billiard table. Geologists believe they formed through crystallization in marine sediments millions of years ago.

Continue to Kokala's colored clays—Jurassic-era formations in purple and ochre hues. The contrast with the surrounding white limestone is striking. Paleontologists have found fossils of sea urchins and belemnites here, tens of millions of years old.

Mount Sherkala ("Lion's Castle") is a solitary mountain with near-vertical walls rising from the flat plain. Depending on the angle, it resembles either a giant yurt or a reclining lion. The circular walk is about 3 km—look for marine fossil imprints in the limestone rocks at its base.

Continue to Airakty-Shomanay valley—jagged rock ridges emerging from the flat desert like the ruins of ancient fortresses. The local name means "Valley of Castles" and the comparison is justified. These formations resulted from millions of years of wind erosion on the Ustyurt Plateau limestone.

Head to Tuzbair salt lake—a dazzling white expanse at the foot of ochre cliffs. The salt forms natural geometric patterns that shift with the light. On clear days, the reflection creates a spectacular mirror effect. Arrive early morning or late afternoon—at midday, the glare is blinding.

Cross the Ustyurt Plateau toward Kyzylkup valley, nicknamed "Tiramisu" by locals because the hills with horizontal colored bands evoke layers of the Italian dessert. The hues alternate between white, pink, cream, and brown. Seasoned travelers compare it to a blend of Arizona's The Wave and Peru's Rainbow Mountains.

Sunrise over Boszhira—set your alarm, it's worth it. The white cliffs emerge progressively from the morning mist like a movie set revealing itself. Experienced photographers say Mangystau never gives the same light twice.

Drive to Beket-Ata underground mosque, about 40 km from Boszhira. This pilgrimage site carved into the cliff in the 18th century honors a Kazakh Sufi sage. The descent to the mosque follows a stairway cut into the rock. The interior is austere but the acoustics are remarkable. It's Mangystau's most visited sacred site—you'll meet Kazakh families who've come from across the country.

activities.text: Camp in the desert—pitch your tent at sunset and enjoy Kazakhstan's clearest starry sky. The total absence of light pollution in Mangystau makes the Milky Way visible to the naked eye.
Free hike around the Airakty formations—there are no marked trails, you're alone in the world. In a full day here, you'll probably encounter no one. Bring everything you need.
Explore Kyzylkup valley on foot—there are no trails, just walk between the striped hills. A drone (if you have one) reveals the full beauty of the patterns from above. Camp in the valley at sunset.

In late afternoon, explore the surroundings of Beket-Ata on foot—the nearby gorges shelter medieval necropolises and carved stone funerary stelae (koitas). The silence of the Ustyurt desert here is total—you literally hear the wind passing between the rocks.

days_plan.itin_title: Kazakhstan Road Trip: Mangystau, Caspian Sea & the Silk Road
Kazakhstan Road Trip: Mangystau, Caspian Sea & the Silk Road
Kazakhstan Road Trip: Mangystau, Caspian Sea & the Silk Road
Kazakhstan Road Trip: Mangystau, Caspian Sea & the Silk Road

map_keywords: Airakty Shomanay Valley of Castles Mangystau

visits.text: Flight Aktau → Shymkent (about 2 hours with layover or direct depending on carrier). Shymkent is Kazakhstan's third-largest city and the gateway to the southern Silk Road. The central bazaar is one of Central Asia's liveliest—spices, fabrics, dried fruits, and cured meat overflow from the stalls.

Stroll through Abay Park, the green lung of downtown. Restaurant terraces line the shaded pathways—the atmosphere is more relaxed than in Astana or Almaty. Shymkent has retained an authentic Central Asian character that the two major cities have lost.

Day trip to Turkestan (170 km, about 1.5 hours on perfect asphalt). The Mausoleum of Khoja Ahmed Yasawi is Kazakhstan's most important monument—UNESCO-listed. Commissioned by Timur in 1389 to replace an older 12th-century mausoleum, it remained unfinished when the conqueror died in 1405. The main dome measures 18 m in diameter—one of the largest in Central Asia. Inside, a 2-ton bronze cauldron (the tai-kazan) stands in the main hall.

Explore the underground mosque of Khoja Ahmed Yasawi, accessible with the same ticket. The complex also includes a small museum of ancient baths. The entire quarter around the mausoleum has been redesigned with the Ethno-Park that recreates traditional Silk Road architecture.

Return to Shymkent (about 3 hours' drive). If you have time before the flight, return to the central bazaar for last-minute shopping—spices, honey, kurt, and dried fruits travel well. Kazakh felts (shyrdak) also make excellent souvenirs but check your luggage space.

activities.text: Taste southern Kazakh cuisine—more spiced and more influenced by Uzbekistan than in the rest of the country. Lagman (hand-pulled noodle soup) and samsa (meat-filled pastries) are local specialties. The kebab restaurants in the old quarter are worth seeking out.

Wander around Turkestan near the mausoleum—Kazakh pilgrims circle the monument counterclockwise, touching the walls and murmuring prayers. It's a living place, not a frozen museum. Return to Shymkent late afternoon.
Departure flight from Shymkent International Airport—daily connections to Almaty (1 hour) and Astana (2 hours), as well as international flights to Istanbul and Dubai.

days_plan.itin_title: Kazakhstan Road Trip: Mangystau, Caspian Sea & the Silk Road
